Two Artificial Analysis charts highlighting the performance shift from GLM-5.1 to GLM-5.2.
The top chart is a bar graph of the "Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index." A purple arrow points from the GLM-5.1 bar (score 40) up to the much higher GLM-5.2 bar (score 51), which sits just behind GPT-5.5 (xhigh).
The bottom chart is an "Intelligence vs. Cost per Intelligence Index Task" scatter plot. A purple arrow maps the transition from GLM-5.1 to GLM-5.2, showing that while GLM-5.2 gained a significant boost in intelligence (moving up to a score of 51), its cost per task also increased, pushing it out of the highlighted green "Most attractive quadrant" to roughly $0.45 per task.

With that we kept on toward the physics building. Then I spotted Feynman in the distance. I had spent the last couple of days on the lookout for Feynman, hoping to manufacture a natural way to bump into him and see if he would still talk to me. I told Constantine I’d see him later. I walked over toward Feynman.

When I got to him, Feynman was gazing at a rainbow. He had an intense look on his face, as if he were concentrating. As if he had never seen one before. Or maybe as if it might be his last.

I approached him cautiously.

“Professor Feynman. Hi,” I said.

“Look, a rainbow,” he said without looking at me. I was relieved that I didn’t detect any residual annoyance in his voice.
2 bikes and bike with wings flying above them along a beach

Two data visualizations from Artificial Analysis comparing AI models. The top bar chart, titled "Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.1," ranks models by performance score. Claude Fable 5 leads with a score of 60, followed by Claude Opus 4.8 at 56, GPT-5.5 (xhigh) at 55, and GLM-5.2 at 51. A purple arrow draws a comparison from the top model down to GLM-5.1 at a score of 40. The lowest-ranked model is gpt-oss-120b at 24. 

The bottom scatter plot, titled "Intelligence vs. Cost per Intelligence Index Task," plots the performance score against the cost per task in USD on a logarithmic scale from $0.03 to $3. A light green shaded rectangle marks the "Most attractive quadrant" for high performance and low cost. Highly cost-effective models like DeepSeek V4 Pro, DeepSeek V4 Flash, and MiMo-V2.5-Pro sit inside or near this green zone at under $0.05 per task. A purple arrow highlights a performance leap from GLM-5.1 up to GLM-5.2. Top-tier frontier models like Claude Fable 5, Claude Opus 4.8, and GPT-5.5 sit in the far top-right, showing the highest intelligence but at a significantly higher cost of $1 to over $3 per task.

Line chart titled “The scissors: total release firehose vs frontier cadence (per quarter),” spanning Q2 2024 to Q2 2026. An orange line, “Total tracked releases (all variants),” climbs steadily from about 12 per quarter to about 82. A blue line, “Frontier-generation releases,” stays flat along the bottom, wobbling between 1 and 3 per quarter and slightly declining by the end. The two lines start close together and fan apart into a widening gap, like opening scissors.
